rationale.summary: The walkthrough shows a broad replacement of the old first-N log rate limiter with a worker-authoritative random LogSampler spanning request, access, and diagnostic logs. It also describes configuration, protobuf propagation, access recorder migration, deployment/docs updates, and expanded validation coverage. No human transcript was available, so the PR's alignment with the original request cannot be determined from conversation intent.

keyPoints.point: The PR replaces the old log rate limiter with a worker-authoritative random sampler across request, access, and diagnostic logging.
keyPoints.snippet: This PR replaces the old first-N log rate limiter with a worker-authoritative random LogSampler spanning request, access, and diagnostic logs

keyPoints.point: The operator-facing configuration changes from a quota setting to sample-rate controls.
keyPoints.snippet: The old per-second `log_rate_limit` setting is replaced with three double sample-rate controls.

keyPoints.point: The propagation protocol now carries sampler configuration in register and heartbeat responses.
keyPoints.snippet: The shared-memory protobuf gains `LogSampleConfigPb` and carries it in register and heartbeat responses.

keyPoints.point: Access logging call sites migrate to lazy recorder facades to avoid work when sampling drops an event.
keyPoints.snippet: AccessRecorder is reshaped around Object, Stream, and RequestOut recorder facades that skip field materialization when sampling says not to record.

critique_ledger.title: Shutdown frees sampler snapshots while log hot paths can still hold raw pointers
critique_ledger.rationale: Log hot paths load `snapshot_` as a raw pointer and then dereference it without any reader lifetime guard. During shutdown, this line deletes the current snapshot immediately after an atomic exchange, so any concurrent LOG/PLOG/AccessRecorder path that already loaded the pointer can dereference freed memory and crash during worker or client teardown.

critique_ledger.title: Avoid copying the formatted request message for every access log
critique_ledger.rationale: Each emitted access log now calls FormatAccessReqMsg(), which returns a std::string before appending it to logStream. In the common non-sampled-marker case that helper returns reqMsg by value, adding another allocation/copy of the already formatted request payload on every foreground client/worker access-log path.

critique_ledger.title: Every sampler config change retains another snapshot until shutdown
critique_ledger.rationale: Each distinct config update pushes the previous snapshot into `oldSnapshots_` and nothing frees those entries until process shutdown. A worker or client receiving repeated dynamic sampling updates will leak memory monotonically, which can eventually exhaust memory in a long-running service.

critique_ledger.title: Dynamic sampler flag transaction is untested
critique_ledger.rationale: The new dynamic update path validates sampler flags as a batch, mutates the process gflags, rolls back on failure, and passes explicit-state bits into LogSampler. The diff only changes flags_test.cpp formatting and exercises sampler config directly, so regressions in Flags::UpdateFlagParameter, unchanged-value explicit marking, multi-flag derivation blocking, or rollback after a bad dynamic update would not be caught.

critique_ledger.title: Non-owning recorder setters need an explicit lifetime contract
critique_ledger.rationale: The new fluent facade exposes `ObjectKeyRef`, `ObjectKeysRef`, `RemoteClientId`, and similar setters that store `string_view` values or container pointers in recorder state, while the same recorder type is also moved through async/shared ownership paths. Future call sites can easily choose a `*Ref` setter for a temporary or shorter-lived object because the header does not document which setters borrow and which own, making the API hard to maintain safely as more access-log fields are added.

routed_questions.Operator sampling configuration: Does the changed configuration hunk preserve a compatibility path for existing `LogRateLimit`, `log_rate_limit`, `logRateLimit`, or `DATASYSTEM_LOG_RATE_LIMIT` users while adding request/access/diagnostic sample-rate controls?
routed_questions.Sampler core logging pipeline: Does the changed sampler snapshot lifetime hunk prevent concurrent logging hot paths from dereferencing a snapshot that shutdown or config update has already reclaimed?
routed_questions.Sampler propagation protocol: Does the changed protobuf hunk avoid breaking old clients that expect `RegisterClientRspPb.log_rate_limit` as field 24 with the old int32 wire type?
routed_questions.Access recorder migration: Do the changed access recorder facade hunks make borrowed field lifetimes safe for every migrated call path, especially paths that move recorders through async or shared ownership?

per_cohort.routed_question: Does the changed configuration hunk preserve a compatibility path for existing `LogRateLimit`, `log_rate_limit`, `logRateLimit`, or `DATASYSTEM_LOG_RATE_LIMIT` users while adding request/access/diagnostic sample-rate controls?
Does the changed sampler snapshot lifetime hunk prevent concurrent logging hot paths from dereferencing a snapshot that shutdown or config update has already reclaimed?
Does the changed protobuf hunk avoid breaking old clients that expect `RegisterClientRspPb.log_rate_limit` as field 24 with the old int32 wire type?
Do the changed access recorder facade hunks make borrowed field lifetimes safe for every migrated call path, especially paths that move recorders through async or shared ownership?
